780	as the construction of a quotient
781	$GL_{d,\Z}\backslash V$, where $V$ is the scheme of all
782	$d\times r$ matrices of rank $d$, and the group-scheme
783	$GL_{d,\Z}$ acts on $V$ on the left by matrix multiplication. However,
784	we will not use the language of group-scheme actions 
785	here, instead, we give a direct elementary construction of 
786	the Grassmannian scheme.
787	
788	The reader can take $d=1$ in what follows, in a first reading, to
789	get the special case $\grass(r,1) = \PP^{r-1}_{\Z}$, which 
790	has another construction as $\proj \Z[x_1,\ldots,x_r]$. 
791	
792	
793	
794	\medskip
795	
796	
797	
798	\textbf{Construction by gluing together affine patches }
799	For any integers $r\ge d\ge 1$, the Grassmannian scheme
800	$\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$, together with the tautological 
801	quotient $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ where \
802	$\U$ is a rank $d$ locally free sheaf on $\grass(r,d)$, 
803	can be explicitly constructed as follows.
804	
805	
806	
807	If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and 
808	$I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal 
809	to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will 
810	mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
811	$I$. 
812	
813	
814	For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$, 
815	consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
816	$X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
817	entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$. 
818	Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
819	$x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically 
820	isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$. 
821	
822	For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let 
823	$P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
824	where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
825	Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
826	the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
827	the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
828	on $U^I_J$. 
829	
830	For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism 
831	$\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
832	defined as follows. The images of the variables 
833	$x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
834	$\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$.
835	In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
836	so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$.
837	
838	Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and 
839	we leave it to the reader to verify that 
840	for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of 
841	cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition 
842	$\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
843	is satisfied. Therefore the schemes 
844	$U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the 
845	${r\choose d}$ different 
846	subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
847	can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
848	finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
849	isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
850	$\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$.
851	
852	
853	
854	\medskip
855	
856	\textbf{Separatedness }
857	The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
858	can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ 
859	defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, 
860	and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme.
861	 
862	
863	\medskip
864	
865	\textbf{Properness }
866	We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. It is enough to
867	verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
868	Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
869	$\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by 
870	a ring homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed
871	one such $I$, next choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum,
872	where $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes 
873	the discrete valuation. As $P^I_I = 1$, note that
874	$\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore
875	$f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$ and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$
876	lies in $GL_d(\K)$.
877	
878	Now consider the homomorphism
879	$g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
880	$$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
881	Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
882	and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
883	$\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$. 
884	As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
885	$\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$. 
886	Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
887	factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of
888	schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
889	$\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked
890	separatedness of $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that 
891	$\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. 
892	
893	
894	\medskip
895	
896	
897	
898	\textbf{Universal quotient }
899	We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
900	together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
901	On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism 
902	$u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
903	by the matrix $X^I$. Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
904	for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
905	$(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
906	by putting 
907	$$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$
908	This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$, 
909	so we get a surjective homomorphism 
910	$u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
911	
912	
913	\medskip
914	\textbf{Projective embedding }
915	As $\U$ is given by the transition functions $g_{I,J}$ described above, 
916	the determinant line bundle 
917	$\det(\U)$ is given by the transition functions
918	$\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J \in GL_1(U^I_J)$.
919	For each $I$, we define a global section 
920	$$\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\grass(r,d),\det(\U))$$ 
921	by putting $\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I \in 
922	\Gamma(U^J,{\mathcal O}_{U^J})$ in terms of the trivialization over the open cover
923	$(U^J)$. 
924	We leave it to the reader to verify that the sections $\sigma_I$ form
925	a linear system which is base point free and separates points 
926	relative to $\spec \Z$, and so gives an embedding of 
927	$\grass(r,d)$ into $\PP^m_{\Z}$ where $m = {r\choose d}-1$.
928	This is a closed embedding by the properness of 
929	$\pi:\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$. In particular, $\det(\U)$
930	is a relatively very ample line bundle on $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$.
931	
932	
933	\medskip
934	
935	{\footnotesize
936	\textbf{Note } The $\sigma_I$ are known as the Pl\"ucker coordinates, 
937	and these satisfy certain quadratic polynomials known as the Pl\"ucker 
938	relations, which define the projective image of the Grassmannian.
939	We will not need these facts. }%end of footnotesize

940	
941	
942	
943	
944	
945	
946	
947	
948	
949	\section{Castelnuovo-Mumford Regularity}
950	
951	
952	
953	Mumford's deployment of $m$-regularity led to a simplification in the 
954	construction of Quot schemes. The original construction of Grothendieck 
955	had instead relied on Chow coordinates. 
956	
957	
958	
959	
960	Let $k$ be a field and let $\F$ be a coherent sheaf on the 
961	projective space $\PP^n$ over $k$. Let $m$ be an integer.
962	The sheaf $\F$ is said to be \textbf{$m$-regular} if we have
963	$$H^i(\PP^n, \F(m-i)) =0\mbox{ for each }i \ge 1.$$
964	The definition, which may look strange at first sight, is suitable
965	for making inductive arguments on $n=\dim(\PP^n)$
966	by restriction to a suitable hyperplane. If $H\subset \PP^n$ is
967	a hyperplane which does not contain any associated point of $\F$,
968	then we have a short exact sheaf sequence
969	$$0 \to \F(m-i-1) \stackrel{\alpha}{\to} \F(m-i) \to \F_H(m-i)\to 0$$
970	where the map $\alpha$ is locally given by multiplication with
971	a defining equation of $H$, hence is injective.
972	The resulting long exact cohomology sequence
973	$$\ldots \to H^i(\PP^n, \F(m-i)) \to H^i(\PP^n, \F_H(m-i)) 
974	\to H^{i+1}(\PP^n, \F(m-i-1)) \to \ldots$$
975	shows that if $\F$ is $m$-regular, then so is its restriction $\F_H$
976	(with the \textit{same value\,} for $m$) 
977	to a hyperplane $H\simeq \PP^{n-1}$ which does not contain any 
978	associated point of $\F$.
979	Note that whenever $\F$ is coherent, the set of associated points of
980	$\F$ is finite, so there will exist at least one such hyperplane $H$
981	when the field $k$ is infinite.
982	
983	The following lemma is due to Castelnuovo, according to 
984	Mumford's {\sl Curves on a surface}.
985	
986	\begin{lemma}\label{Castelnuovo} 
987	If $\F$ is an $m$-regular sheaf on $\PP^n$ then
988	the following statements hold:
989	
990	\textbf{(a)} The canonical map $H^0(\PP^n,{\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}(1))\otimes 
991	H^0(\PP^n,\F(r)) \to H^0(\PP^n,\F(r+1))$ is surjective
992	whenever $r\ge m$.
993	
994	\textbf{(b)} We have $H^i(\PP^n, \F(r))=0$ whenever $i \ge 1$ and $r\ge m-i$.
995	In other words, if $\F$ is $m$-regular, then it is $m'$-regular for all
996	$m' \ge m$.
997	
998	
999	\textbf{(c)} The sheaf $\F(r)$ is generated by its global sections, 
1000	and all its higher cohomologies vanish, whenever $r\ge m$.
1001	\end{lemma}
1002	
1003	\proof As the cohomologies base-change correctly under a field extension, 
1004	we can assume that the field $k$ is infinite.
1005	We argue by induction on $n$. The statements
1006	\textbf{(a)}, \textbf{(b)} and \textbf{(c)} clearly hold when $n=0$, so
1007	next let $n\ge 1$. 
1008	As $k$ is infinite, there exists a hyperplane $H$ 
1009	which does not contain any associated point of $\F$, so that
1010	the restriction $\F_H$ is again $m$-regular as explained above.
1011	As $H$ is isomorphic to $\PP^{n-1}_k$, by the 
1012	inductive hypothesis the assertions of the lemma hold for the sheaf $\F_H$.
1013	
1014	When $r=m-i$, the equality $H^i(\PP^n, \F(r))=0$ in statement \textbf{(b)}
1015	follows for all $n\ge 0$ by definition of $m$-regularity. 
1016	To prove \textbf{(b)}, we now proceed by induction on $r$ where 
1017	$r\ge m-i+1$. Consider the exact sequence 
1018	$$H^i(\PP^n, \F(r-1)) \to H^i(\PP^n, \F(r)) \to 
1019	H^i(H, \F_H(r))$$
1020	By inductive hypothesis for $r-1$ 
1021	the first term is zero, 
1022	while by inductive hypothesis for $n-1$
1023	the last term is zero, which shows that the middle term is zero, 
1024	completing the proof of \textbf{(b)}. 
1025	
1026	Now consider the commutative diagram
1027	{\footnotesize%scriptsize
1028	$$\begin{array}{lccc}
1029	& H^0(\PP^n, \F(r))\otimes H^0(\PP^n,{\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}(1))& 
1030	\stackrel{\sigma}{\to}& H^0(H, \F_H(r))\otimes H^0(H, {\mathcal O}_H(1)) \\
1031	& ~\dna {\scriptstyle \mu}& & ~\dna {\scriptstyle \tau}\\
1032	H^0(\PP^n, \F(r))~~~~ \stackrel{\alpha}{\to} & H^0(\PP^n, \F(r+1))
1033	& \stackrel{\nu_{r+1}}{\to} & H^0(H, \F_H(r+1))
1034	\end{array}$$
1035	}
1036	The top map $\sigma$ is surjective, for the following reason: 
1037	By $m$-regularity of $\F$ and using the statement \textbf{(b)}
1038	already proved, we see that 
1039	$H^1(\PP^n, \F(r-1))=0$ for $r\ge m$, and so the restriction map
1040	$\nu_r: H^0(\PP^n, \F(r)) \to H^0(H, \F_H(r))$ is surjective. 
1041	Also, the restriction map
1042	$\rho : H^0(\PP^n,{\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}(1)) \to H^0(H, {\mathcal O}_H(1))$ is surjective. 
1043	Therefore the tensor product $\sigma = \nu_r \otimes \rho$ 
1044	of these two maps is surjective. 
1045	
1046	The second vertical map $\tau$ is surjective by inductive
1047	hypothesis for $n-1 = \dim(H)$.
1048	
1049	Therefore, the composite $\tau\circ \sigma$ is surjective,
1050	so the composite $\nu_{r+1}\circ\mu$ is surjective,
1051	hence $H^0(\PP^n, \F(r+1)) = \im(\mu) + \ker(\nu_{r+1})$.
1052	As the bottom row is exact, we get 
1053	$H^0(\PP^n, \F(r+1)) =  \im(\mu) + \im(\alpha)$. 
1054	However, we have $\im(\alpha) \subset \im(\mu)$, as the map
1055	$\alpha$ is given by tensoring with a certain section of 
1056	${\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}(1)$ (which has divisor $H$). 
1057	Therefore, $H^0(\PP^n, \F(r+1)) = \im(\mu)$.
1058	This completes the proof of \textbf{(a)} for all $n$.
1059	
1060	To prove \textbf{(c)}, consider the map $H^0(\PP^n, \F(r))
1061	\otimes H^0(\PP^n,{\mathcal O}_{\PP^n}(p)) \to
1062	H^0(\PP^n, \F(r+p))$, 
1063	which is
1064	surjective for $r\ge m$ and $p\ge 0$ as follows from 
1065	a repeated use of \textbf{(a)}. 
1066	For $p\gg 0$, we know that 
1067	$H^0(\PP^n, \F(r+p))$ is generated by its global sections.
1068	It follows that $H^0(\PP^n, \F(r))$ is also generated 
1069	by its global sections for $r\ge m$. We already know from \textbf{(b)}

546	\textbf{(2) } \textbf{Grassmannian as a Quot scheme } 
547	For any integers $r\ge d\ge 1$, an explicit construction
548	the Grassmannian scheme
549	$\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$, together with the tautological 
550	quotient $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ where \
551	$\U$ is a rank $d$ locally free sheaf on $\grass(r,d)$, 
552	has been given at the end of this section. A proof of the 
553	properness of $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is given there, 
554	together with a closed embedding $\grass(r,d)\hra 
555	\PP(\pi_*\det\U) = \PP^m_{\Z}$ where $m={r \choose d} - 1$.
556	
557	Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient 
558	$u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the 
559	contravariant functor 
560	$$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$ 
561	from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
562	set of all equivalence classes 
563	$\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
564	where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
565	Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$ 
566	exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$.
567	
568	
569	\textbf{Grassmannian of a vector bundle} 
570	Show that for any ring
571	$A$, the action of the group $GL_r(A)$ on the free module $\oplus^r A$ 
572	induces an action of $GL_r(A)$ on 
573	the set $\Grass(r,d)(A)$, such that for any ring homomorphism $A\to B$,
574	the set-map $\Grass(r,d)(A)\to\Grass(r,d)(B)$ is equivariant with respect
575	to the group homomorphism $GL_r(A) \to GL_r(B)$. (In schematic terms,
576	this means we have an action of the group-scheme $GL_{r,\Z}$ on 
577	$\grass(r,d)$.) 
578	
579	Using the above show that, more generally, if $S$ is a scheme and 
580	$E$ is a locally free ${\mathcal O}_S$-module of rank $r$, 
581	the functor $\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}$ 
582	on all $S$-schemes which 
583	by definition associates to any $T$ 
584	the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients  
585	$q: E_T \to \F$ where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$,
586	is representable. The representing scheme
587	is denoted by $\grass(E,d)$ and is called the rank $d$ 
588	relative Grassmannian of $E$ over $S$.
589	It parametrises a universal quotient $\pi^*E \to \F$
590	where $\pi : \grass(E,d) \to S$ is the projection.
591	Show that the determinant line bundle $\bigwedge^d \F$ on
592	$\grass(E,d)$ is relatively very ample over $S$, 
593	and it gives a closed embedding
594	$\grass(E,d) \hra \PP(\pi_* \bigwedge^d \F) \subset \PP(\bigwedge^d E)$. 
595	(The properness of the embedding follows from the 
596	properness of $\pi : \grass(E,d) \to S$, which follows locally
597	over $S$ by base-change from  properness of 
598	$\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$ -- see Exercise \textbf{(5)}
599	or \textbf{(7)} below.)
600	
601	
602	\textbf{Grassmannian of a coherent sheaf} 
603	If $E$ is a coherent sheaf on $S$, not necessarily locally free, 
604	then by definition the functor $\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}$ 
605	on all $S$-schemes associates to any $T$ 
606	the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients  
607	$q: E_T \to \F$ where $\F$ is a locally free on $T$ of rank $d$.
608	If $r: E' \to E$ is a surjection of coherent sheaves on $S$, then show that
609	the induced morphism of functors $\Grass(E,d)\to \Grass(E',d)$,
